title: When markets get less structured, does AI lose its edge?
author: Disastrous_Hotel_574
selftext:
Something I’ve been thinking about recently:

In periods where the market feels less structured — messy ranges, weak follow-through, inconsistent moves — even human decision-making becomes harder.

Which raises an interesting question for AI systems:

👉 Do they lose effectiveness when the market becomes less “clean”?

Most models (rule-based or learned) rely on some form of:

* structure
* repeatable patterns
* or stable conditions

But when those disappear:

* signals become less reliable
* noise increases
* confidence drops

So maybe the real challenge isn’t building a better model, but:  
👉 knowing when the model shouldn’t be used.

Curious to hear from people here:

Have you noticed your systems performing differently in less structured markets?

comments.author: rainhunter007
comments.body:
when you say less “clean” you’re really talking about increased variance. markets are heteroskedastic which means that their volatility is not statistically “normal”. when variance increases, absolutely, most models generally suffer unless they’ve been built to deal with changes in variance.

from my experience, it’s a bit of both: yes, models suffer with changes in the character of variance but it also depends on the model and what it exploits.

your comment on “knowing when the model shouldn’t be used” is true and in the context of algorithmic trading is really just putting rules on the model to trade in favorable regimes. automated regime classification itself is an entire subfield of study, and there are models that try to predict variance like GARCH.

it’s kinda like weather. would you start mowing your lawn in a thunderstorm? would you scatter seeds in the wind? would you go for a hike in a dust storm?would you have a picnic in the pouring rain? any model has to have some knowledge of the environment it trades just like you have to know the weather when you plan an outdoor activity.

replies.author: Disastrous_Hotel_574
replies.body:
That’s a great way to frame it — especially the link with heteroskedasticity.

I like the idea that it’s not really about the model “failing”, but about the environment shifting outside of what the model was built to exploit.

And yeah, the weather analogy fits perfectly. Most systems aren’t wrong — they’re just being applied in conditions where their assumptions no longer hold.

The regime classification part is where things get really interesting. Because in practice, even if you model variance (GARCH, etc.), the challenge is less about measuring it and more about deciding:  
at what point does the environment become “untradeable” for your system?

That threshold seems highly dependent on the edge itself.

Curious how you handle that — do you rely on explicit regime filters (volatility thresholds, etc.), or more implicit signals based on performance degradation?
